course_description: Flow of funds & financial markets, theory & behaviour of interest rates, term & risk structure of interest rates, exchange rates, interest parity, expectations formation, equity markets, debt market, Euro markets, derivatives markets, social allocation of capital.

requirements.assumed_background: The course ECON3210 Financial Markets and Institutions is intended for students who contemplate a deep and quite technical understanding of financial markets and institutions. It is essential therefore that students who take this course have been exposed to financial topics in previous courses. Please check the pre-requisites listed above, which should be completed before enrolling in this course. It is not recommended to take the pre-requisite courses during the same semester as ECON3210.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Additional Timetable Information
The Thursday workshop will commence in Week 1. We will use the first session to complete topic 1 on Financial Intermediation. From week 2 the workshop will be devoted to training exercises on the topic of the previous week.

A guest lecturer Max Morley from Westpac will give a talk on 16th October 2012 . The attendance is compulsory and will be recorded for this event.

Book into your diary NOW the following possible dates for the mid-term test: Saturday 1st, 8th, or 15th September 2012 . The mid-term test is managed centrally by the University. The exact date will be announced by the University in mid-August. Please keep all these dates free of any other commitments for the time being.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: This course aims to provide students with a thorough understanding of the current issues and debates concerning financial markets and institutions. The course has a focus on applied issues and encourages students to refer to many sources of information such as websites of financial institutions and financial publications so that students can acquire a greater hands-on knowledge of the real world of finance. The course aims further to develop students analytical and technical skills and to encourage lateral thinking.

learning_outcomes.description: identify why financial markets and financial intermediaries are crucial to a well functioning economy.
understand how the financial system in the U.S. is both similar to and different from financial systems in other countries.
represent financial intermediaries operations through their T-accounts.
realize to what extent asymmetric information explains the way our financial system is structured.
distinguish the different types of conflicts of interest in the financial industry and discuss policies to remedy them.
calculate and interpret some banks performance indicators.
show how banks statements are affected by defaulting loans and devalued assets.
describe the process of securitization by lenders.
understand the benefits and dangers associated with securitisation.
apply the basics of interest gap analysis.
describe the operations that could increase or decrease the interest rate risk exposure of a bank.
describe the recent changes in international regulation on bank capital adequacy
understand the effect of the new capital adequacy regulation on the risk taking behaviour of banks.
understand the practice and implications of short-selling.
describe the changes in the regulation of short-selling.
understand the conflicts of interest in the mutual funds industry.
understand how late and timing trade can lead to fraud.
distinguish between mutual funds and hedge funds.
understand why and how most central banks currently set a target for the overnight interest rate.
describe how central banks contribute to the stability of the financial markets.
describe how central banks have altered their tools for implementing monetary policy during the recent financial crisis.
describe the various causes of the subprime mortgage crisis.
understand how a financial crisis can spread across countries and sectors.
understand factors contributing to default on sovereign debt.
analyze the contagion effects of a sovereign debt default.

assessment_summary.category: Exam - Mid Semester Outside Scheduled Class
assessment_summary.title: Mid semester exam

assessment_details.title: Mid semester exam
assessment_details.category: Exam - Mid Semester Outside Scheduled Class
assessment_details.format: Multiple-choice, Short answer, Short essay, Problem solving
assessment_details.task_description:
The mid-term exam will take place outside the lectures and tutorial slots, on Saturday 1st, 8th or 15th September 2012 , and will be administered by the University. The exact date will be communicated to the School around mid-August 2012, and students are urged to keep the three dates free of any other commitments for the time being. Students who are working during weekends should already inform their employer about these three dates, as work commitments will not be considered as a valid extenuating circumstance for not attending the exam . The exam will last 90 minutes plus 10 minutes perusal.

The topics included in the exam will depend on the actual date for the mid-term exam. The principle that will apply to define the contents of the exam will be any material from the beginning of the semester up to the topic lectured two weeks before the exam with the corresponding tutorial the week before the exam.

Be aware that students entitled to a deferred mid-term exam will have to take that exam on the Thursday two weeks after at a time to confirm.

assessment_details.title: Training
assessment_details.task_description:
This test comprises 20-25 questions and will last 60 minutes.

This test does not count towards the final mark but is compulsory. The test has been designed to give a chance to students to become familiar with the technology involved in online tests and to get a feeling of the style of the questions they could expect. Students should undertake this training test in the same conditions as the ones that will prevail for the actual tests, i.e. with the same computer, the same Internet connection and in the same room.

IMPORTANT NOTICE Students who do not undertake this compulsory training test will not be entitled to extenuating circumstances related to technical problems that may occur during the later tests that are part of the formal assessment.

learning_resources.required_resources: To ensure that the course uses the most updated information, the lecturer will communicate the reading list for each topic in advance of each lecture and will provide all compulsory readings via Blackboard. As an indication of the nature of these readings, please see below in Section 3.5 the reading list that was required in the previous academic year.
learning_resources.recommended_resources: The recommended reading will be provided at the lecture to ensure that students use up-to-date information. ALL readings are available on Blackboard.
learning_resources.university_learning_resources:
Access to required and recommended resources, plus past central exam papers, is available at the UQ Library website ( http://library.uq.edu.au/search/r?SEARCH=ECON3210 ).

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myServices website ( https://student.my.uq.edu.au/ ).
learning_resources.school_learning_resources:
From Semester 1, 2011 The School of Economics has adopted the new University Calculator Scheme. For assessment in this course students will be permitted to use Casio FX82 series (82 with any letters) or university approved (labelled) calculators . For further details on approved calculators http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/exam-calculators .

Calculator requirements may differ for other university courses and students should check each course profile for the specific requirements.

PLEASE NOTE: It is the students responsibility to ensure they have the correct calculator prior to assessment in each of their courses. Students without the correct calculator in a School of Economics exam may have their calculator confiscated.

Guidelines for correct referencing techniques can be found in the School of Economics publication Guide for Assignment Presentation , which can be accessed on the web site www.uq.edu.au/economics
learning_resources.other_learning_resources:
The course will mainly rely on recent articles published by Financial newspapers, central banks, financial regulators, financial markets operators and international organisations like IMF, OECD or BIS. For each topic, students will be provided with a list of required and recommended readings. Most of them will be available on the Web and a link to these documents will be possible directly from Blackboard. The reading list for a given topic will be made available the week when it is taught in order to include in it the most relevant articles available at that time. As an illustration of the type of reading expected in the course and the level of technicity, please find below the reading list that accompanied the course in 2011.

You will be able to access the lecture slides via "Blackboard" no later than 12:00 on the Sunday before the lecture. An e-mail will inform the students when the slides have been put online.

All lectures are recorded with Echo360 and the recording is available on Blackboard the week after the lecture.

ECON3210 does not include tutorials and the lecturer will provide handouts with questions (often accompanied by model answers) to provide the students with an opportunity to train and self-assess. Some online training quizzes made of MCQ, true-false questions and numerical questions will also be available for each topic to prepare for the online tests that are part of the formal assessment.

ECON3210 Financial Markets and Institutions

Reading List 2011

Be aware that the 2012 reading list may include more recent references and will remove some of the references below.

Topic 1 Financial Intermediation

Miskhin (2010) chapter 2 Overview of the Financial System in Financial Markets and Institutions, Pearson, Global Edition, Ninth Edition.

Bracoud (2011) Financial transactions, financial intermediation and players balance sheets

RBA (2011) Developments in the structure of the Australian financial system

RBA (2010) Main types of financial institutions

RBA (2010) Ownership of Australian equities and corporate bonds

Topic 2 Economic Analysis of the financial structure

Mishkin (2010) chapter 8 Economic Analysis of Financial structure in Money, Banking and Financial Institutions, 9thedition, Global edition, Pearson.

Utzig, S (2010) The Financial Crisis and the Regulation of Credit Rating Agencies: A European Banking Perspective. ADBI Working Paper 188.

RBA (2009) Australian Corporates Sources and Uses of funds, Bulletin, October 2009.

Topic 3 Mark-to-Market and commercial banks financial statements

Hogan, Avram and others (2004) Chapter 2 Understanding financial statements of ADIs in Management of Financial Institutions, 2ndedition, Wiley

McMahon, G. (2011) Mark-to-Markets real role in the crisis, the CPA Journal, February.

RBA (2010) Recent developments in banks funding costs and lending rates, Bulletin, March

RBA (2010) Recent trends in Australian Banks‟ issuance of bonds, Bulletin, March

RBA (2010) The Australian Government Guarantee Scheme, Bulletin, March

Topic 4 Securitisation and other structured finance

IMF (Sept 2008) What is securitization? By Andreas Jobst

RBA (January 2008) The asset-backed commercial paper market, Bulletin.

RBA (2009) Whither securitisation?

RBA speech (2010) The state of Play in the Securitisation market MP3 available

Topic 5 Commercial banks interest rate risk management

Fraser, Gup and Kolari (2007) chapter 5 An Overview of Asset/Liability Management in Commercial Banking: the Management of Risks

Topic 6 Bank capital adequacy regulation

APRA (2008) The implementation of Basel II in Australia, APRA Insight, Issue 2

Cannata and Quagliariello (2009) The role of Basel II in the subprime financial crisis: guilty or not guilty?

APRA (2011) Basel III the journey and the destination

RBA (2011) Basel III and beyond

Topic 7 Security lending and short lending

ASIC (2011) Regulatory guide 196 Short Selling.

ASLA Website tab entitled About securities lending

ASX( March 2008) ASX public consultation paper Short Selling.

Parliamentary Library Bills Digest number 65 2008-09

Topic 8 The challenges of the managed funds industry

Hull (2010) ch4 Mutual Funds and Hedge Funds in Risk Management and Financial Institutions.

RBA (2011) The Exchange-Traded Funds, Bulletin, March 2011.

RBA (2006) Recent (!!!) developments in Australian hedge funds industry, Bulletin, November 2006.

Topic 9 New implementation of monetary policy

Mishkin and Eakins (2009) chapter 8Conduct of Monetary Policy‟ in Financial Markets & Institutions, Pearson.

RBA (June 2003) The Reserve Bank‟s Open market operations

RBA (Oct 2008) Market operations in the last year. Speech by Guy Debelle

BIS (July 2008) Central Banks operations in response to the financial turmoil, section 3.

FRBNY (January 2010) Federal Reserve Commercial Paper Funding Facility, Staff report 423

RBA (2010) Fifty years of monetary policy: what have we learnt?

Topic 10 The subprime mortgage crisis

Blundell-Wignall and Atkinson (2008) The subprime crisis

Brunnermier(2008) Deciphering the 2007-2008 Liquidity and credit crunch

RBA (2009)The Global Financial Crisis: Causes, Consequences and Countermeasures

Mishkin (2010) chapter 9 Financial crisis in developed economies in The Economics of Money, Banking and financial Markets

Fcic (2011) Conclusions of the financial crisis inquiry commission

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the Universitys assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity
It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism
The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

Medical grounds: Applications for extension on medical grounds shall be made by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and supporting documentation at the location outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course concerned.

Exceptional circumstances: Applications for extension on the grounds of exceptional circumstances shall be made to the relevant Course Coordinator/Program Director by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and a personal statement outlining the grounds for the application at the location and by the due date outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course in which this application is made. If the exceptional circumstances are such that the student cannot reasonably be expected to have complied with these conditions, a case should be made as to why these conditions could not be met.

Outcome of application: Students will be advised of the outcome of their application via their student email.

Feedback on Assessment
Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.02-assessment )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

Feedback in this Course

Apart from the formal BEL couse evaluation at the end of the course, you are encouraged to express your concerns or suggestions at any time. We shall devote the first 5 minutes of each lecture for such informal feedback.

School of Economics
Assessment Guidelines

School of Economics Assessment Guidelines

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses
Students on a placement course also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-placement-courses ) for detailed information.

Working with Children
Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25004 ) to find out how to apply for a blue card.

Students with a Disability
Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety
Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Other Course Guidelines

A significant part of the formal assessment for this course (30%) takes the form of online tests run at home. While students are allowed to use their textbooks and lecture notes, resorting to the help of a third party is strictly forbidden when doing the online tests.

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
ECON3210 Financial Markets and Institutions is in the core course list for the Bachelor of Economics Major International Trade and Finance and in the Bachelor of Commerce Major Finance (starting in 2012). Note that the students in the Bachelor of Commerce who started before 2012 have the opportunity to choose ECON2200 Management of Financial Institutions instead. ECON3210 is an elective course for all Majors in the Bachelor of Economics.

The course aims to provide a deep and thorough understanding of the financial markets and institutions. The main aim of this course is to present material that is of great relevance for the understanding of the current financial environment. For that purpose, all topics selected this year will have made (and likely will still do) the headlines of the financial newspapers in the last twelve months. We will cover in detail, among other topics, the subprime-mortgage crisis, the European sovereign debt crisis, Fed quantitative easing and the debates for reforming regulation in financial markets in general and bank capital adequacy regulation in particular.

The topics covered this year will be the following:

Financial Intermediation and its future

An Economic Analysis of Financial Structure

Mark-to-market accounting and default crisis in banks financial statements

Securitisation

Bank Interest-rate risk Management

Reforms of Basel capital adequacy regulation

Short-Selling

The Challenges of the Managed Funds Industry

New Implementation of Monetary Policy

The subprime mortgage crisis and its consequences

The Sovereign debt crisis

Counterparty risk: its management and the new regulation

The level of technicality and the topics targeted require that some general knowledge on financial institutions and markets be acquired beforehand. It is essential that the pre-requisites requirements are met before the course starts. In particular it is not recommended to take ECON2200 during the same semester as ECON3210 . No traditional textbook currently available on the market would give the appropriate coverage required for this course. The course therefore mainly relies on recent articles published by financial newspapers, central banks, financial regulators, financial markets operators and international organisations like IMF, OECD or BIS. ALL reading resources are made available through Blackboard. In the absence of a single textbook, the slides for the course are much more elaborated and detailed than usual and have to be considered as handouts for the study compiling the main ideas of the reading.

Lectures are recorded with Echo360 (formerly called Lectopia), and the files are released at the end of each week. Each topic covered in the lectures is the subject of a workshop the week after, followed by training exercises and self-assessed quizzes. With a week delay the model answers to the workshop questions are provided on Blackboard. The course makes an intensive use of Blackboard, with the upload of many resources (slides, workshop and training handouts, model answers, extra reading), the availability of quizzes online as well as a vibrant discussion board organised by topic and supervised by the tutors and the lecturer. This year for the first time we will use a virtual classroom on Blackboard, in which the lecturer will provide online consultation in addition to the usual face-to-face sessions.

Dr Frédérique Bracoud, the course coordinator and lecturer for the sixth consecutive year, is a theoretical banking Microeconomist, trained in France and Belgium, and has a record of 12 year-experience in teaching Financial Economics at introductory and advanced levels in the UK and in Australia. She implemented major changes 4 years ago and is now confident that the course has the right structure. As a result, no major adjustments will be made this year, and one can expect a smooth operation in spite of the large audience involved (the enrolment is expected to be three times larger this year!).

As students like to know how the previous cohort performed in this course the year before, the grade distribution for 2011 is provided in the table below. The data account for the special and supplementary exams results and exclude the students who have withdrawn from the course. This is a course where very good students can secure the highest grade as illustrated by the large proportion of 7 (more than 14%) in the table below. It should be flagged that unfortunately a significant proportion of students (more than 6%) also failed the course last year.

Please be aware that the course applies a slightly different grading system from the one in other courses. In addition to the traditional criterion that applies on the average mark across assessments, the course grading system also imposes some requirements on the performance in some particular pieces of assessment: either the mid-term exam OR the final exam must be in the range of the grade too. For more details please refer to the assessment part of the course profile and to the handout entitled "The Grading System in ECON3210" available on Blackboard under the heading "Assessment" .

Since it has been redesigned in 2009, the course ECON3210 Financial Markets and Institutions has been well received by students. Last year the course was rated 4.18/5 and the lecturer 4.28/5. Students who liked the course over the years often mention 1) the relevance of the material and how up to date the information provided was and 2) the lecturers enthusiasm and knowledge of the subject. The introduction this year of a one hour workshop will meet the needs for training exercises in the absence of tutorials.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

The relevance of the material covered during the lectures to understand the current affairs and the enthusiasm of the lecturer for the subject were among the positive aspects that came in the students evaluations this year, Some aspects of the course however were a source of frustration for some students. Please find a summary of the praises and issues that appeared in last year's evaluation, as well as a description of the way Frederique will address the problems.

Like

Do not like

Actions for 2012

Interesting and relevant contents

Slides on Blackboard posted the evening before lectures, too late to print them and read them

Commitment to put them online by Sunday lunchtime, more than 48 hours before the lecture

Up-to-date information on current affairs

Absence of tutorials

The School has introduced this year a third hour consisting in a workshop, during which the lecturer solves some training questions.

Extensive use of Blackboard, large selection of learning material

Too much reading
The lecturer will trim a bit the reading list this year.
However the students should be aware that the course will still remain demanding in that respect.

Detailed and clear explanations of complex concepts

Mid-term exam results available too late

The lecturer had a team of only 10 tutors for marking more than 1000 scripts across three courses, hence the delay. This year external markers will be asked to help and markers will be required to mark on Campus to increase the rotation speed of the scripts.

Continuous assessment and flexibility of online tests

Self assessment opportunities

Passionate, knowledgeable and very approachable lecturer.

Awful/ adorable French accent

No action! In absence of consensus the lecturer will keep her accent for another year! :-)

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Undergrad Pass) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A1 . A comprehensive and well-founded knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A4 . An understanding of how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
C1 . The ability to work and learn independently .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C4 . The ability to identify problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
D1 . The ability to define and analyse problems .
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D3 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions.
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E2 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .

other_learning_activities_information:
The lectures will be supported by slides, elaborating topics from the main textbook and providing background information as well as additional explanations. Attendance is not a formal requirement, but highly recommended. Echo360 recording is available at once. Be aware of the dangers of not attending lectures and relying on Echo360: firstly one cannot be entirely sure that there will not be any technical problem making the recording inoperational and secondly the students may postpone the listening too much and lost track.

Workshops reinforce the material seen at the lectures and take place the week after the lecture. Attendance to the workshop is compulsory. Detailed model answers are available on the week end that follows the workshop.

If students have enquiries about the contents of the course and did not manage to have an answer during lectures and workshops, they have access to many other venues to raise their questions. The Blackboard discussion board is the first place where questions easy to formulate should be directed. The discussion board is organised through forum, one for each topic of the course. The lecturer should be able to reply within 48 hours in week days. Students can also help each other and a gift (to be defined) will go to the student who has helped the others in the most efficient way. If the question is hard to write then the consultation with the lecturer is the right venue to raise the question.

Previous 3. Resources

Next 5. Assessment
course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

No understanding of lecture material, and assigned reading.

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

Deficient understanding of lecture material, and assigned reading.

A weighted average performance over all assessments in the range 20.00-44.99%

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

Superficial or partial or faulty understanding of lecture material, and assigned reading.

Option 1 A weighted average performance over all assessments in the range 45.00-49.99%

Option 2 A weighted average performance over all assessments in the range 50.00-64.99% AND the performance in the mid-term exam and the final exam below 50.00%.

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Adequate understanding and correct statement of facts and propositions, does not cover all major details and gives analyses, syntheses and critique containing some major deficiencies. Makes some errors but shows evidence of a basic knowledge of lecture material, and assigned reading.

Option 1 A weighted average performance over all assessments in the range 50.00-64.99% AND the performance in either the mid-term exam or the final exam equal or above 50.00%.

Option 2 A weighted average performance over all assessments in the range 65.00-74.99% AND the performance in the mid-term exam and the final exam below 65.00%.

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Sound statement of facts and propositions and gives analyses, syntheses and critique containing no major deficiencies. Makes no significant errors and shows evidence of a sound to good knowledge of lecture material, and assigned reading.

Option 1 A weighted average performance over all assessments in the range 65.00-74.99% AND the performance in either the mid-term exam or the final exam equal or above 65.00%.

Option 2 A weighted average performance over all assessments in the range 75.00-84.99% AND the performance in the mid-term exam and the final exam below 75.00%.

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

Provides an accurate and complete statement and gives clear and consistent analyses, syntheses and critique. Makes no significant errors and shows evidence of a sound to good knowledge of lecture material, and assigned reading.

Option 1 A weighted average performance over all assessments in the range 75.00-84.99% AND the performance in either the mid-term exam or the final exam equal or above 75.00%.

Option 2 A weighted average performance over all assessments in the range 85.00-100% AND the performance in the mid-term exam and the final exam below 85.00%.

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

Provides accurate and complete statements and gives clear and consistent analyses, syntheses and critique. Makes no errors and shows evidence of a complete knowledge of lecture material, and assigned reading.

A weighted average performance over all assessments in the range 85.00-100% AND the performance in either the mid-term exam or the final exam equal or above 85.00%.

Other Requirements & Comments:

The "weighted average performance over all assessments" used in the definitions above is calculated with the weight of 10% for each online test, 30% for the mid-term exam and 50% for the final exam as stipulated in the summary at the top of the page.

A detailed description of the grading system with many examples and with the rationales for using these grading criteria is provided in the lecturer's handout "The grading system in ECON3210" available on Blackboard under Assessment.
late_submission:
The online tests are not available on Blackboard after the due date and therefore a late submission is irrelevant for the online tests. If a student fails to do the online test by the due date, the mark 0 is allocated to the piece of assessment.

There is no opportunity to get a second chance for the test after the due date. If a student has a medical certificate covering the whole period of the availability of the test online or any other valid reason (as defined by the University regulation) for not taking the test, he/she should inform the lecturer as soon as possible before the due date and a specific arrangement will be considered on a case-by-case basis.
other_assessment_information:
The School of Economics is committed to reducing the incidence of plagiarism. Further information on plagiarism and how to avoid an allegation of plagiarism is available under Section 6. Policies and Guidelines - 6.1. Assessment Related Policies & Guidelines of this Course Profile.

DEFERRED EXAMINATIONS

If you are unable to attend a scheduled exam (e.g. due to illness or other unavoidable circumstances which can be evidenced - e.g. medical certificate), you may be eligible to sit a deferred examination at a later time.

Information about deferred examinations can be found on the web at http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2995 and also on the School of Economics website

at http://www.uq.edu.au/economics/policy-on-absences-from-mid-semester-examinations Please read BOTH these websites very carefully.

All applications for deferred exams must be submitted within (5) calendar days of the original date of the examination.

Mid-semester exam (SCHOOL exam ) - submit your application to the Course Administrator Christina Ellsworth with appropriate documentation.

End of Semester Examination (CENTRAL exam) - submit your application to the Student Centre, Level 1, J.D. Story Building with appropriate documentation.

SUPPLEMENTARY EXAMINATIONS

If you fail a course (generally with a grade of three), you can find information about supplementary examinations at: http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2954 . Read this information very carefully to see if you may be eligible to apply for a supplementary examination. Also see General Award Rules, Section 1A.17.3, and your program rules.

Applications for supplementary exams must be lodged online via mySI-net, within 5 days of the official release of results.

Important Information regarding Supplementary Examinations for courses within the Faculty of Business, Economics & Law:

· Supplementary Examinations are weighted at 100% (i.e. progressive assessment does not count) and therefore ALL modules and topics are assessable.

· Results for Supplementary Examinations cannot be graded higher than a four (4).

· Once approved, applications cannot be rescinded.

· Students are advised to take the Deferred & Supplementary Exam period into account when making post-examination plans (12-14 December).
